1: 'Hear this word that I take up over you in lamentation, O house of Israel.' The shift from accusation (rib, lawsuit) to lamentation (qinah) is a literary signal: Amos now adopts the funeral-lament meter (the distinctive 3+2 stress pattern of Hebrew funeral poetry) to sing Israel's dirge before it dies. This is the form in which ancient Near Eastern professional mourners lamented the dead — Amos performs the funeral as a prophetic act before the event, which is more terrifying than the accusation because it presupposes the verdict is already final.
2: 'Fallen, no more to rise, is the virgin Israel; forsaken on her land, with no one to raise her up.' The 'virgin Israel' (betulat Yisrael) — the nation personified as a young woman who has not yet been married/destroyed — is portrayed as already fallen in the prophetic vision. The lament is in past tense in the Hebrew, a prophetic perfect expressing certainty: it is as though it has already happened. The specific phrase 'no one to raise her up' (ein meqimah) refers to the absence of a kinsman-redeemer who would restore an Israelite woman to social standing — YHWH is not performing that role here because the judgment has been determined.
3: 'For thus says the Lord GOD: The city that marches out a thousand will have a hundred left, and the one that marches out a hundred will have ten left to the house of Israel.' The decimation-by-nine-tenths is a catastrophic attrition rate — only 10% survival from military engagement. This is not a metaphor for partial defeat but for virtually total annihilation. The specific numbers (1000 → 100; 100 → 10) follow the Deuteronomic curse pattern of progressive reduction (Deuteronomy 28:62: 'you will be left few in number, whereas you were as numerous as the stars in the sky').
4: 'For thus says the LORD to the house of Israel: Seek me and live (dershuni vichyu).' After the funeral lament, YHWH's invitation to life is startling and merciful: the judgment is not yet sealed for those who will turn. The word darash (seek) is the covenant-seeking term that appears throughout the prophets (Deuteronomy 4:29; Jeremiah 29:13; Hosea 10:12); it describes the active, intentional orientation toward YHWH that is the response to the prophetic call. 'Seek me and live' is structurally similar to John 5:24 and Amos 10:10 — finding YHWH means life; finding his cultic substitutes means death (v5).
5: 'Do not seek Bethel, do not go to Gilgal, do not journey to Beersheba.' The three pilgrim sanctuaries — Bethel (Jacob's stairway, Genesis 28:10-22), Gilgal (the first camp after crossing the Jordan, Joshua 4:19-24), and Beersheba (Abraham's covenant with Abimelech, Genesis 21:31) — are all associated with foundational covenant events. YHWH's command not to seek him at these places is the prophetic claim that the cult at these sites has become an obstacle to finding YHWH rather than a means of access. 'Gilgal will surely go into exile, and Bethel will come to nothing' — the plays on words (Hagilgal galoh yigleh; Beit-el yihyeh le'aven) make Gilgal a wordplay on 'go into exile' and Bethel into 'Beth-aven' (House of Wickedness, cf. Hosea 4:15).
6: 'Seek the LORD and live, or he will sweep through the house of Joseph like a fire, and it will devour, with no one to quench it, for Bethel.' The 'house of Joseph' — Ephraim and Manasseh, the two dominant northern tribes — stands as a synecdoche for the entire northern kingdom. The fire that 'devours Bethel' (the main northern sanctuary) with no one to quench it is the covenant-curse fire of total judgment (cf. Amos 1:4-2:5 where YHWH sends fire on every condemned nation). The link between seeking YHWH and avoiding the fire is direct: the sanctuary they worship at will be burned; only YHWH himself — not the cult site — can provide protection.
7: 'You who turn justice into wormwood and hurl righteousness to the ground!' Wormwood (la'anah) is a bitter, toxic plant (Artemisia absinthium) used throughout the OT as an image of bitter judgment (Jeremiah 9:15; Amos 23:15; Lamentations 3:15,19; Revelation 8:11). The perversion of justice into something toxic and the casting of righteousness to the ground is a spatial reversal of how justice should work: justice should be raised up and celebrated; instead it is thrown down and poisoned. This indictment (v7) forms an inclusio with the justice-exhortation (v24: let justice roll down like waters) around the central doxology (vv8-9).
8: 'He who made the Pleiades and Orion, who turns midnight into dawn and darkens the day into night' — the second of Amos's doxologies (cf. Amos 4:13; Amos 9:5-6), celebrating YHWH's cosmic power over the created order. The Pleiades (Kimah) and Orion (Kesil) were well-known star clusters in the ancient Near East; in Akkadian astronomical texts, Orion was associated with the god of chaos. YHWH's control over these constellations asserts his sovereignty over the forces that other cultures considered independent deities. The alternation of dawn and darkness reflects YHWH's sovereignty over the day/night cycle — the same darkness Amos uses for the Day of the LORD imagery (Amos 5:18-20).
9: 'He flashes destruction on the stronghold and brings ruin upon the fortress.' The same YHWH who created the Pleiades and Orion also destroys fortifications — the cosmic Creator is also the destroyer of human military infrastructure. This direct connection between creation theology and judgment theology is characteristic of 8th-century prophecy (cf. Isaiah 40:12-26 where creation sovereignty underpins YHWH's ability to judge nations). The fortress/stronghold language (meteg/mibtsar) echoes the fortress vocabulary of the nation-oracle series (Amos 1-2), tying the doxology to the judgment framework.
10: 'They hate the one who speaks up in court (mochiach bashaer) and despise the one who tells the truth.' The gate (sha'ar) was the site of the ancient Israelite legal system — elders sat at the gate to adjudicate disputes (Ruth 4:1-12; Deuteronomy 16:18). The person who 'reproves in the gate' is both the elder who gives legal verdicts and the prophet who speaks truth publicly. The hatred of those who speak honestly in legal proceedings reflects the corruption of the justice system documented elsewhere in Amos (Amos 2:6-8; Amos 8:4-6). The connection between the prophetic role and the legal role is significant: Amos is doing in public what the corrupt judges refuse to do at the gate.
11: 'Therefore because you trample on the poor and take from them exactions of grain, you have built houses of dressed stone, but you will not live in them; you have planted pleasant vineyards, but you will not drink their wine.' The covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:30-31 ('you will build a house, but you will not live in it; you will plant a vineyard, but you will not eat its fruit') is applied with specific precision to the economic exploitation Amos has described. The 'houses of dressed stone (batei gazit)' are the ashlar-construction luxury residences confirmed in Samaria's archaeological record — more expensive and impressive than the standard rubble construction of ordinary houses.
12: 'For I know how many are your crimes and how great are your sins — you who afflict the righteous, take bribes, and turn aside the needy in court.' YHWH's direct knowledge of the specific offenses (ki yadati rabbim pish'eichem) removes any pretense of legal process: YHWH himself is both witness and judge. The three specific offenses — afflicting the righteous (tzadikim), taking bribes (kofer), and deflecting the needy at the gate — are the classic forms of juridical corruption in the ancient Israelite legal system, each explicitly prohibited in the Mosaic law (Exodus 23:2-8; Deuteronomy 16:19).
13: 'Therefore the prudent will keep silent in such a time, for it is an evil time.' The 'prudent person' (maskil) who keeps silent is not indifferent but tactically quiet — in a context where the powerful control the courts (vv10-12), the wise person knows that public speech is futile and dangerous. The 'evil time' (ki et ra'ah hi) is a diagnosis of the moment's moral condition: it is not merely dangerous to speak but structurally evil. This verse may be a wry self-commentary by Amos on the situation of a prophet who is commanded to speak in exactly such an evil time.
14: 'Seek good and not evil, so that you may live; and so the LORD, the God Almighty, will be with you, just as you say.' The phrase 'just as you say' (ka'asher amartem) is the sharp irony: Israel claims YHWH is with them (cf. Micah 3:11: 'Is not the LORD among us? Disaster will not come upon us'), but the claim is empty unless it is grounded in the seeking of good and avoidance of evil. The conditional form of the promise ('so that... will be with you') makes clear that the divine presence Israel presumes is actually contingent on the moral orientation Amos demands.
15: 'Hate evil, love good, and establish justice in the gate; it may be that the LORD, the God Almighty, will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph.' The 'remnant of Joseph' (she'erit Yosef) is the first hint in Amos of a possible survival — a remnant preserved through the judgment. The 'may be' (ulai) is characteristically prophetic hedging: the possibility of grace, not its guarantee. 'Establishing justice in the gate' is the structural demand — reforming the legal institutions that Amos has indicted is the concrete expression of 'seeking good.'
16: 'Therefore this is what the LORD, the God Almighty, says: There will be wailing in all the public squares and cries of anguish in all the streets; farmers will be called to mourn and professional mourners to wail.' The professional mourners (mesofedim) and 'those skilled in lamentation' (yode'ei nehi) reflect the well-developed mourning industry of the ancient Near East, where funeral rituals were elaborate public events requiring skilled performers. ANE mourning texts (from Mesopotamia and Egypt) document the range of mourning practices — wailing, tearing garments, shaving heads — that Amos references. The invocation of the farmers to mourn is the inversion of the harvest festival: instead of joy at the harvest, there will be mourning at the loss of everything.
17: 'In all the vineyards there will be wailing, for I will pass through your midst, says the LORD.' 'I will pass through (avarti bekirbecha)' echoes the Passover narrative: the same formula used in Exodus 12:12 (YHWH passing through Egypt to strike the firstborn) is now directed at Israel. The reversal is total: the Passover was the event that demonstrated YHWH's judgment was against Egypt and protection was for Israel; now YHWH's 'passing through' brings judgment to Israel as it once did to Egypt.
18: 'Woe to you who desire the day of the LORD! Why do you want the day of the LORD? It is darkness, not light.' The 'Day of the LORD' (yom YHWH) had developed in Israel's theology as the expected day of divine intervention on Israel's behalf — a day of YHWH's victory over Israel's enemies. Amos's reversal is devastating: those who long for the Day of the LORD as the day of national triumph are longing for their own judgment. The concept of the Day of the LORD likely originated in the tradition of YHWH as divine warrior (cf. the 'day of Midian', Isaiah 9:4; the 'day of battle', Zechariah 14:3), representing YHWH's decisive defeat of his enemies.
19: 'It will be as though someone fled from a lion only to meet a bear, or went home and rested his hand on the wall only to have a snake bite him.' The triple impossibility of escape — from lion, from bear, from the house itself — mirrors the impossibility-of-flight list in Amos 2:14-16. The specific animals (lion, bear, snake) are all dangers present in ancient Canaan's agricultural and pastoral landscape; the progression from outdoor danger (lion in the open) to outdoor danger (bear nearby) to indoor danger (snake in the house itself) closes every possible place of refuge. There is nowhere the Day of the LORD does not reach.
20: 'Is not the day of the LORD darkness, not light — pitch-dark, without a ray of brightness?' The reiteration of the darkness imagery (v18 repeated) closes the Day of the LORD section with emphatic finality: there is no exception, no partial brightness, no 'ray of brightness' (nogah lo bo, no shining in it). The absolute character of the darkness — not merely dim but 'pitch-dark' (afelah velo nogah) — corresponds to the completeness of the judgment that Amos has been describing throughout the book.
21: 'I hate, I despise your feasts, and I take no delight in your solemn assemblies (azaroteichem).' The solemn assemblies (atzeret/azarah) were the closing festivals of the major pilgrimage feasts (Leviticus 23:36; Deuteronomy 16:8). YHWH's rejection of the entire cultic calendar — 'I hate... I despise (sane'ti mas'ati)' — uses the strongest possible language of divine rejection. This is not a moderate criticism of cultic abuse but a comprehensive repudiation of the cult as it is actually being performed. The same dynamic appears in Isaiah 1:11-15 and Jeremiah 7:21-23.
22: 'Even though you offer me your burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them; and the offerings of well-being of your fatted animals I will not look upon.' The three major sacrifice categories — burnt offerings (olah), grain offerings (minchah), and well-being offerings (shlemim) — are explicitly rejected. These were the three primary forms of Israelite sacrifice (Lev 1-3); their rejection is the rejection of the entire Levitical sacrificial system as it is actually being practiced at Bethel and Gilgal. YHWH 'will not look upon (lo abit)' the fatted animals — refusing to acknowledge even the costliest offerings.
23: 'Take away from me the noise of your songs; I will not listen to the melody of your harps.' The musical component of Israelite worship — the Levitical singing and instrumental accompaniment that was developed under David and maintained in the Temple tradition (1 Chronicles 25:1-7; Ps 150) — is also rejected. The 'noise' (hamon) of songs and the 'melody' (zimrat nevalekha) of harps are the cultic music that accompanied the sacrifices. YHWH's rejection of both sacrifice and music is the rejection of the entire multi-sensory experience of Israelite worship. The positive counterpart comes in v24.
24: 'But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream (venagar kamayim mishpat utzdaqah kenahal eitan).' The most famous verse in Amos — and one of the most famous in all prophetic literature — posits the alternative to the rejected cult. The water metaphors are not merely aesthetic: in the semi-arid Levant, a 'perennial stream' (nahal eitan, a wadi that flows year-round rather than seasonally) was precious and rare. Most wadis were seasonal, drying up in summer. YHWH wants justice that flows like a perennial spring — constant, reliable, not seasonal or occasional. This verse was quoted by Martin Luther King Jr. in the 'I Have a Dream' speech (1963), the most famous modern appropriation of any OT prophetic text.
25: 'Did you bring me sacrifices and offerings forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?' The rhetorical question revisits the wilderness tradition with a challenging subtext: the sacrificial cult as it was practiced at Bethel was not the original form of Israel's relationship with YHWH. The wilderness period — when there was no elaborate temple cult — was nonetheless the period of most intimate YHWH-Israel relationship (Jeremiah 2:2-3: 'I remember the devotion of your youth... you followed me in the wilderness'). The question implies that the cult at Bethel is a historical accretion that has displaced the covenantal relationship it was supposed to express.
26: 'You carried Sikkuth your king and Kiyyun your star-god, your images that you made for yourselves.' This verse contains one of the most textually difficult passages in Amos; 'Sikkuth' and 'Kiyyun' are probably Assyrian astral deity names (possibly Sakkuth/Saturn and Kewanu/Saturn's star in the Akkadian tradition), with the vowels replaced by the Hebrew vowels for shiqquts (detestable thing) — a standard scribal technique for expressing contempt for foreign gods. The reference to astral worship reflects the Assyrian cultural influence on northern Israel during this period, when Assyrian religious iconography spread through trade and political contact.
27: 'Therefore I will take you into exile beyond Damascus, says the LORD, whose name is the God Almighty.' The closure of the first major section of Amos with the threat of exile 'beyond Damascus' anticipates the Assyrian deportation of 722-720 BCE, when the northern kingdom's population was deported to various locations in the Assyrian Empire (2 Kings 17:6: Halah, Habor, the river of Gozan, and the cities of the Medes — all well beyond Damascus to the northeast). The specification 'beyond Damascus' may reflect Amos's knowledge of the Assyrian deportation routes, which went through Damascus to Mesopotamia. The divine title closing the chapter — 'whose name is the God Almighty' — frames the judgment with YHWH's sovereign authority over all the armies that will enact it.
